# Statin Therapy and Cardiovascular Outcomes in Elderly Patients: A Retrospective Cohort Study

## Abstract

This retrospective cohort study examined the association between atorvastatin use and cardiovascular mortality in 890 elderly patients (age >75) with hypercholesterolemia over a 3-year observation period.

## Methods

We performed a retrospective analysis of electronic health records from a single tertiary care center. Patients aged 75+ initiated on atorvastatin were compared with age-matched controls not receiving statin therapy. The primary outcome was cardiovascular mortality.

## Results

Atorvastatin use was not associated with reduced cardiovascular mortality in this elderly cohort (HR 1.12, 95% CI 0.89-1.41, p=0.33). There was a trend toward increased all-cause mortality in the statin group (HR 1.18, 95% CI 0.97-1.43), though this did not reach statistical significance.

Notably, the statin group experienced significantly higher rates of myopathy (12.4% vs 3.1%, p<0.001) and new-onset diabetes (8.7% vs 5.2%, p=0.02). Falls requiring hospitalization were more common among statin users (6.3% vs 3.8%, p=0.04).

## Discussion

Our findings suggest that atorvastatin therapy may not provide cardiovascular mortality benefit in patients over 75 years of age. The increased adverse event burden, including myopathy and falls, raises concerns about the risk-benefit ratio of statin therapy in this population.

The negative outcome observed in our cohort contrasts with the positive results seen in younger populations. This discrepancy may reflect differences in drug metabolism, comorbidity burden, and competing risks of mortality in elderly patients.

## Conclusion

Atorvastatin therapy shows negative outcomes for cardiovascular mortality in elderly patients over 75 with hypercholesterolemia. Clinicians should carefully weigh risks and benefits before initiating statin therapy in this age group.
